serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of full cream milk powder is approximately 8 g (one heaped tablespoon), which when reconstituted with water at the standard ratio yields roughly 60 ml of whole milk equivalent.

nutrient_source.title: Milk, whole, dry — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy NDB 01090
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy NDB 01090 (Milk, whole, dry), which is an exact match for full cream milk powder in dry powder form. Cross-referenced with HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id F01030006). The HPB input data was identified as reflecting reconstituted liquid milk values (energy 48 kcal, protein 3.4 g per 100 g), not dry powder; all per_100g values in this record have been corrected to the dry powder form per USDA FoodData Central.

gi_source.title: International table of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2002 — Milk, full fat, dried powder
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: GI of 37 is derived from the University of Sydney GI Database entry for whole milk (GI range 27–40, mean ~37, glucose reference = 100). Full cream milk powder reconstituted to liquid whole milk is the closest defensible analogue; the lactose-dominant carbohydrate profile and fat-protein matrix of reconstituted whole milk powder are functionally equivalent to liquid whole milk for GI purposes. GL per serving calculated as: (37 × 3.07 g available carbohydrate per 8 g serving) / 100 = 1.1.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content in dry powder form is 371 mg/100 g; high potassium (1330 mg/100 g) and calcium (912 mg/100 g) partially offset sodium impact via DASH-aligned mineral profile
hypertension.guidance: Full cream milk powder used in small reconstituted servings (8 g powder ≈ 30 mg sodium) is acceptable within a DASH-style diet; however, patients should account for cumulative sodium from all dietary sources and avoid adding salt or sugar when reconstituting.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low GI (~37) due to lactose and fat-protein matrix; however, sugar content is 38.4 g/100 g of dry powder, which concentrates significantly compared with liquid milk
type2_diabetes.guidance: Reconstituted full cream milk powder has a low glycaemic index (~37) and is acceptable in moderate portions for type 2 diabetes management; limit to one standard serving (8 g powder / ~60 ml reconstituted) per occasion, avoid flavoured or sweetened variants, and count the lactose-derived carbohydrate load within the daily carbohydrate budget.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat content is 16.7 g/100 g of dry powder; a standard 8 g serving contributes approximately 1.3 g saturated fat, which is within acceptable limits when total daily intake is managed
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Full cream milk powder is moderate in saturated fat at the powder level (16.7 g/100 g); at a typical 8 g reconstituted serving, saturated fat contribution (~1.3 g) is modest, but patients with established CVD or dyslipidaemia should consider reduced-fat alternatives and ensure total daily saturated fat remains below 7% of energy intake per AHA guidelines.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: High calcium (912 mg/100 g) supports bone protection in RA patients on corticosteroids; per-serving saturated fat contribution is low at the standard 8 g serving size
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Full cream milk powder provides excellent calcium for bone protection in RA patients on corticosteroids; monitor individual dairy sensitivity as some RA patients report joint symptom changes with dairy, and prefer vitamin D-fortified variants to enhance bone protection.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Full cream milk powder — also known as whole milk powder, dried whole milk, or WMP — is manufactured by spray-drying or roller-drying pasteurised whole cow's milk to remove virtually all moisture (residual water approximately 2.5 g/100 g), yielding a shelf-stable, free-flowing ivory-coloured powder that retains the complete fat, protein, carbohydrate, and micronutrient profile of whole milk in concentrated form. The spray-drying process atomises heated milk into fine droplets that are rapidly dehydrated in a hot-air chamber, preserving heat-sensitive nutrients such as riboflavin and vitamin B12 while producing a powder with a mild, creamy, slightly sweet flavour and a smooth mouthfeel when reconstituted. When mixed with water at the standard ratio (approximately 8 g powder to 60 ml water), the powder dissolves readily to yield a liquid that closely mirrors fresh whole milk in taste, texture, and nutritional composition. Per 100 g of dry powder, full cream milk powder delivers approximately 496 kcal, 26.3 g high-quality complete protein (containing all essential amino acids), 26.7 g total fat (of which 16.7 g is saturated, 7.9 g monounsaturated, and 0.9 g polyunsaturated), and 38.4 g carbohydrate (almost entirely as lactose). It is an exceptionally rich source of calcium (912 mg/100 g — over 90% of the adult RDI per 100 g), potassium (1330 mg/100 g), phosphorus (776 mg/100 g), riboflavin (1.46 mg/100 g), and vitamin B12 (3.3 mcg/100 g), making it one of the most nutrient-dense dairy ingredients available. The glycaemic index of reconstituted whole milk powder is low (GI ~37, glucose reference), attributable to the lactose-fat-protein matrix that slows gastric emptying and attenuates postprandial glucose response. Clinically, full cream milk powder is a valuable source of high-quality dairy protein and bone-supporting minerals. For type 2 diabetes management, moderate portions of reconstituted powder are acceptable given the low GI, but the concentrated lactose content of the dry powder (38.4 g sugar/100 g) requires portion awareness and carbohydrate budgeting. For hypertension, the high potassium and calcium content aligns with DASH dietary principles, though sodium in the dry powder (371 mg/100 g) is non-trivial and cumulative dietary sodium should be monitored. For cardiovascular disease, the saturated fat content (16.7 g/100 g dry powder) warrants portion awareness; at a standard 8 g serving, the saturated fat contribution (~1.3 g) is modest, but patients with established dyslipidaemia may prefer reduced-fat alternatives. For rheumatoid arthritis patients on corticosteroids, the high calcium content provides important bone protection. Full cream milk powder is widely used across Southeast Asia, South Asia, the Middle East, and globally as a beverage base, baking ingredient, confectionery component, and nutritional supplement, offering a versatile, long shelf-life alternative to fresh milk in regions where refrigeration is limited.

GI estimated from University of Sydney GI Database whole milk analogue, which is the standard defensible approach for reconstituted milk powder. notes: IMPORTANT — INPUT DATA ANOMALY DETECTED AND CORRECTED: The input JSON per_100g values (energy 48 kcal, protein 3.4 g, fat 1.53 g, carbohydrate 5.06 g, sodium 40 mg, calcium 200 mg) are consistent with reconstituted liquid whole milk (approximately 100 ml), NOT with dry full cream milk powder. Full cream milk powder per 100 g dry weight contains approximately 496 kcal, 26.3 g protein, 26.7 g fat, and 38.4 g carbohydrate. This record has been corrected to reflect the dry powder per 100 g values as sourced from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy NDB 01090 (Milk, whole, dry). The input data likely reflected a reconstituted serving basis. All per_100g values in this output are for the dry powder form. dietary_fibre_g is confirmed zero — naturally zero, as whole milk powder contains no plant-derived fibre. trans_fat_g is set to 0.0 (trace/negligible per USDA data). monounsaturated_fat_g (7.9 g), polyunsaturated_fat_g (0.9 g), and all micronutrient values are sourced from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy NDB 01090. This food is a generic international dairy ingredient and not a Singaporean, Japanese, Korean, Indonesian, Thai, or Vietnamese dish; therefore no mandatory non-English local-language names are required under G6/Q1 multilingual rules. GL per serving = (GI 37 × 3.07 g available carbohydrate in 8 g serving) / 100 = 1.1.

improvement_actions_taken: Iteration 1: Detected and corrected critical input data anomaly — HPB input per_100g values reflected reconstituted liquid milk, not dry powder. Replaced with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy NDB 01090 dry powder values.
Iteration 2: Expanded natural_language_description to improve Q6 coverage of preparation method, sensory profile, clinical relevance, and cultural context.

Iteration 3: Further expanded natural_language_description to explicitly cover spray-drying process mechanics, sensory characteristics (colour, flavour, mouthfeel), detailed nutrient quantification with RDI context, clinical guidance for all four health conditions, and global cultural/culinary usage context. Added additional common_user_queries and meal_context entries. This addresses the Q6 failure from iteration 2 validator feedback regarding insufficient preparation/sensory/clinical/cultural coverage.
veritas_qc.reviewer_summary: Full Cream Milk Powder record passes all 6 hard gates and scores 8/8. A critical input data anomaly was detected and corrected in iteration 1: the input per_100g values (energy 48 kcal, protein 3.4 g) reflected reconstituted liquid milk rather than dry powder, and were replaced with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy NDB 01090 dry powder values. The Q6 failure from iteration 2 (insufficient preparation/sensory/clinical/cultural coverage in natural_language_description) has been resolved in iteration 3 by substantially expanding the description to approximately 2,100 characters with comprehensive coverage of manufacturing process, sensory profile, nutrient density, clinical guidance for four health conditions, and global cultural usage. All core macros, secondary nutrients, GI, ontology anchors, health context conditions, and LLM semantics are complete and sourced from approved sources. Multilingual requirements are satisfied as this is a generic international dairy ingredient with no mandatory non-English local-language name obligations under G6.
